title: Annex — Build & host the game
meta.desc: A technical annex: build the Docker image, run the realtime server, and deploy Tower Duel on a host like alwaysdata, a VPS, a container platform or static hosting.
hero.title: Build & host the game
hero.lead: The technical companion to the notebook: how to build the bundle, wrap it in Docker, and deploy it — on alwaysdata, a VPS, a container platform, or plain static hosting.
s0.lead: Tower Duel can be deployed in two very different shapes. Choosing the right one decides everything else on this page.
s0.fig.staticSub: just files — no Node, no server
s0.fig.cap: Left: a folder of static files. Right: a small Node process that serves the page and referees the realtime match.
s0.li1: 🗂️ Static — only the built dist/ folder. Cheapest and simplest (GitHub Pages, Netlify…), but the connected TV + phones mode does not work: there is no server to run the match.
s0.li2: 🖧 Node server — runs server/index.js, which serves the page and hosts the realtime WebSocket rooms. Required for the full multiscreen experience. This is what the Docker image ships.
s0.callout.warn: Key decision. Want phones-as-controllers? You need the Node server (Docker, VPS, alwaysdata, Fly.io…). Just the solo keyboard game? Static hosting is enough.
s1.p1: Everything starts from one command. Vite compiles the source into a self-contained dist/ folder of HTML, JS and assets.
s1.code1.c2: # compile the single-page bundle into dist/
s1.p2: The Vite config uses base: './' (relative paths), so the result is portable: it works whether it's served from a domain root or a sub-path. To preview the built bundle locally:
s1.code2.c2: # full Node server (page + realtime), on http://localhost:3000
s1.callout.tip: Remember. npm start runs node server/index.js and serves dist/, so always npm run build first.
s2.p1: The repository ships a multi-stage Dockerfile. A first stage installs everything and builds the bundle; a second, slim stage keeps only the runtime dependencies and the built files. Smaller image, no build tools in production.
s2.p2: Build the image, then run it:
s2.code2.c1: # build the image (the trailing dot = current folder)
s2.code2.c2: # run it: map host port 8088 to the container's 3000
s2.p3: Open http://localhost:8088. For phones to join they need a reachable address — but inside a container the auto-detected IP is the Docker bridge (e.g. 172.17.0.x), which phones can't reach. Set PUBLIC_HOST to the machine's LAN address (e.g. 192.168.1.20) so the QR code on the TV points somewhere reachable.
s2.callout.nerd: For the curious. The server reads the PORT (default 3000) and the optional PUBLIC_HOST (the host/IP advertised in the QR code) environment variables. The image also declares a HEALTHCHECK that pings /, so orchestrators can tell when the container is actually ready.
s3.p1: On your own server (a $5 VPS is plenty), run the container and put nginx in front to terminate HTTPS and forward traffic. The one thing you must not forget: WebSocket upgrade headers, or the realtime channel silently fails.
s3.code1.c1: # these two lines are what make WebSocket work
s3.p2: Then get a free HTTPS certificate (the browser requires wss:// on an HTTPS page):
s3.callout.warn: Pitfall. On an https:// page the WebSocket must be wss:// (secure). The client builds the URL from the current page, so serving the whole site over HTTPS is enough — no code change needed. Mixing http page + wss (or the reverse) is blocked by the browser.
s4.lead: alwaysdata runs a Node.js application directly, without Docker. The server serves the page and the WebSocket on the same origin — so there's nothing special to configure on the realtime side, and no client change (it builds wss://<host>/ws on its own from the page). No need to set up two separate sites like some tutorials do: a single Node site at the root is enough.
s4.deploy.h: 1) Get the code there (the clean option: the pre-built bundle)
s4.deploy.p: Do not build on the server. Build locally then upload the bundle: the game is already compiled, so alwaysdata only needs express + ws.
s4.code.cap: build locally, then install on alwaysdata
s4.code.c3: # upload the contents of release/tower-duel-<version>/ into ~/www/towerduel
s4.deploy.warn: ⚠️ Pitfall: on alwaysdata the install runs in production mode by default, so npm install / npm ci omits the devDependencies — including vite. If you deploy the raw git repo (without a pre-built dist/) and try npm run build in place, it fails for lack of vite. Two ways out: either the pre-built bundle above (recommended), or force npm ci --include=dev && npm run build to build on the server.
s4.site.h: 2) Create the site (Web → Sites → Add a site)
s4.site.p: In the admin panel, add a site and fill in:
s4.site.li3: Working directory: the folder where dist/ lives (e.g. ~/www/towerduel).
s4.site.li5: Idle time: 0. Without it, alwaysdata puts the app to sleep when idle; a page request wakes it, but a WebSocket upgrade does not wake the backend → the wss fails with “can't establish connection”. Idle time = 0 keeps the process always alive.
s4.port.p: Port: alwaysdata provides the PORT variable and the server already reads it — nothing to change. Domain & HTTPS: attach your (sub)domain, alwaysdata issues the certificate → page and WebSocket served over HTTPS/WSS automatically.
s4.callout.nerd: Why it's simple here. A single Node process answers both the pages and /ws, so no separate WebSocket service and no custom proxy. But: alproxy cuts idle connections after 300 s, and rooms live in memory inside that process. The server sends a heartbeat every 30 s to keep lobby sockets alive; and as long as you stay on a single instance, everyone lands on the same room registry. If the site grows and moves to several processes/instances, two players on different instances will no longer find each other (“room not found”) — you'll then need shared state (Redis…) or sticky sessions.
s5.p1: Managed container hosts deploy the exact same Docker image, with WebSocket support out of the box.
s5.li1: 🪰 Fly.io — fly launch detects the Dockerfile; set the internal port to 3000 in fly.toml, then fly deploy.
s5.li2: 🟪 Render — create a Web Service from the repo, runtime Docker. Render injects PORT and routes WebSocket traffic automatically.
s5.callout.nerd: For the curious. The match lives in memory (no database). That's fine for a game, but it means each instance holds its own rooms — keep it to a single instance, or two players who land on different instances won't find each other.
s6.p1: If you only want the solo, keyboard game, you can host the built files anywhere static — no server bill at all.
s6.code.c1: # then publish the contents of dist/ to your static host
s6.code.c2: # (Netlify: drag & drop dist/, or set build = "npm run build", publish = "dist")
s6.callout.warn: Limitation. Static hosting has no Node process, so the connected TV + phones mode cannot run. The QR/room flow needs the server. For multiscreen, use one of the Node options above.
s6.callout.tip: Note. This very notebook is published with GitHub Pages from the docs/ folder — that's static hosting in action, for documentation rather than the game.
s7.li1: 🔌 Phones connect but the match never starts → the WebSocket isn't getting through. Check the Upgrade / Connection proxy headers.
s7.li2: 🔒 Console error about an insecure WebSocket → your page is HTTPS but the socket is ws://. Serve the whole site over HTTPS so it becomes wss://.
s7.li3: 🚪 Container starts then exits / nothing on the port → the platform's PORT doesn't match what's exposed. Let the app read PORT and map ports correctly.
s7.li4: 📵 Phones can't reach the game on a LAN → you opened localhost, or in Docker the auto-detected IP is the bridge address. Use the machine's LAN IP (shown in the server logs and the QR code) — in a container, set PUBLIC_HOST.
s7.li5: 🧩 404 on refresh of a sub-page → the SPA fallback to index.html is missing. The Node server handles it; for static hosts, add a catch-all rewrite to index.html.
s7.li6: 🔌 “can't establish connection to wss://…/ws” (the handshake fails right away) → the Node backend isn't answering the upgrade. Open https://<host>/healthz: JSON with a pid = Node is running (then check that idle time = 0); the game page or a 404 = the site isn't served by Node (fix the site type / command / working directory).
s7.li7: 🚪 “room not found” while everyone is on the same URL → either the room expired (the TV socket cut by the proxy after 300 s of inactivity — fixed by the heartbeat in ≥ v1.1.x), or the site runs across several instances (keep the process count at 1).
